draft.introduction: William of Ockham’s Summa Logicae (c. 1323) is the foundational text of nominalism. Ockham’s principle of parsimony (“entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity”) dismantled the realist metaphysics of his predecessors and reshaped the trajectory of Western thought.

translation.text: All those who treat of logic try to show that arguments are composed of propositions and propositions of terms. Therefore a term is the fundamental unit of an argument. A term, as it is used here, is a sign which signifies something. Such a sign can be part of a proposition or can be a proposition by itself. But universal terms do not signify universal things, for there are no such things; they signify individual things in a universal manner.
translation.original_text: [Latin: Omnes logicae tractatores intendunt ostendere quod argumenta componuntur ex propositionibus et propositiones ex terminis]

translator_notes.note: Ockham’s nominalism—the denial that universals have any existence outside the mind—was revolutionary. If there are no real universals, then each individual thing relates to God directly and uniquely, not through participation in a shared Form or nature. This had profound implications for the theosis tradition, which depended on the real communicability of the divine nature.
